channel.title: Ilia Klishin Publications Feed
channel.description: Fact-based multilingual digest with original source links.

item.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
item.description:
In this Feb 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
It connects media analysis with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2022/02/14/putin-macron-talks-highlight-gap-in-russia-expertise-a76375

item.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
item.description:
Klishin traces the sequence from mass mobilization to repression: criminal cases, police violence, pressure on media owners, and harassment of public figures.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/12/10/russias-mass-protests-10-years-on-finding-hope-in-apparent-defeat-a75781

item.title: Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions
item.description:
A short summary of media freedom, with a direct link to the original publication.
This piece places media freedom in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/08/24/the-kremlin-is-coming-for-media-one-by-one-and-society-is-helpless-to-stop-it-a74879

item.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
item.description:
How public-opinion narratives are shaped by institutions, incentives, and media distribution logic.
It connects public opinion with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/07/12/the-magic-mirror-of-public-opinion-a74492

item.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
item.description:
A short summary of media ethics, with a direct link to the original publication.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/06/07/the-dilemma-of-the-great-terror-how-to-report-roman-protasevichs-confession-a74125

item.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
item.description:
How election-timing rhetoric can postpone decisions and normalize strategic waiting.
It explains electoral timing and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/05/17/lets-wait-until-after-the-elections-a73915

item.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
item.description:
The piece focuses on elite discourse and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2021.
This piece places elite discourse in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/03/19/luiza-rozova-and-the-helpless-intellectual-a73301

item.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
item.description:
Which technical and political mechanisms can be used to restrict social-network access.
It connects social network regulation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/02/12/four-ways-the-kremlin-could-block-western-social-networks-in-russia-a72920

item.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
item.description:
This The Moscow Times (2021) publication examines comparative media framing and summarizes the main argument.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/01/14/the-capitol-storming-gives-russians-an-escape-from-their-reality-a72598

item.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
item.description:
How platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces.
It explains disinformation and platforms and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/12/18/warning-an-army-of-russian-trolls-has-invaded-tiktok-a72410

item.title: Why Russians Dislike Good Russians in American Films No Less Than Bad Russians
item.description:
The article outlines cultural representation in clear terms and places it in editorial context.
It connects cultural representation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/20/why-russians-dislike-good-russians-in-american-films-no-less-than-bad-russians-a72097

item.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
item.description:
How cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/09/great-russian-literature-versus-one-tweet-by-stephen-king-a71993

item.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
item.description:
The piece focuses on platform influence and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2020.
It explains platform influence and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/09/28/anonymous-telegram-channels-are-shaping-russias-reality-a71588

item.title: Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The
item.description:
Why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning.
This piece places belarus coverage framing in a concrete editorial moment in 2020.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/08/14/why-do-russians-resent-western-media-over-the-protests-in-belarus-a71155

item.title: LA Times coverage referencing TV Rain soldier investigations
item.description:
The article outlines external validation in clear terms and places it in editorial context.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.latimes.com/world/europe/la-fg-russia-soldier-20150914-story.html

item.title: English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism
item.description:
How intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights reporting.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.news24.com/archives/city-press/anti-putin-activist-complains-of-security-harassment-20150429

item.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
item.description:
The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
It explains war-reporting verification and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://tvrain.tv/blog/blog_mihaila_zygarja/nashi_soldaty_vyjasnjaem_sudbu_rossijskih_voennyh_okazavshihsja_na_granitse_s_ukrainoj-435/

item.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
item.description:
This The Moscow Times (2014) publication examines disinformation analysis and summarizes the main argument.
It explains disinformation analysis and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2014/05/21/the-kremlins-trolls-go-west-a35651

item.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
item.description:
The article outlines human rights documentation in clear terms and places it in editorial context.
It connects human rights documentation with the original text published by Human Rights Watch.
Original source: https://www.hrw.org/news/2012/03/01/russia-harassment-critics
